perturbation.original_text: "Subsidiary" shall mean: (i) a corporation, company or other entity, in which a ---------- Party now or hereafter, owns or controls, directly or indirectly, fifty percent (50%) or more of the outstanding shares or securities (representing the right to vote for the election of directors or other managing authority), provided, however, that such corporation, company, or other entity shall be deemed to be a Subsidiary only so long as such ownership or control exists; or (ii) an entity which does not have outstanding shares or securities, as may be the case in a partnership, joint venture or unincorporated association, but in which a Party now or hereafter, owns or controls, directly or indirectly, fifty percent (50%) or more of the ownership interest representing the right to make the decisions for such entity, provided, however, that such entity shall be deemed to be a Subsidiary only so long as such ownership or control exists.
perturbation.changed_text: "Subsidiary" shall mean: (i) a corporation, company or other entity, in which a ---------- Party now or hereafter, owns or controls, directly or indirectly, fifty percent (50%) or more of the outstanding shares or securities (representing the right to vote for the election of directors or other managing authority), provided, however, that such corporation, company, or other entity shall be deemed to be a Subsidiary only so long as such ownership or control exists; or (ii) an entity which does not have outstanding shares or securities, as may be the case in a partnership, joint venture or unincorporated association, but in which a Party now or hereafter, owns or controls, directly or indirectly, **twenty-five** percent (25%) or more of the ownership interest representing the right to make the decisions for such entity, provided, however, that such entity shall be deemed to be a Subsidiary only so long as such ownership or control exists.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by modifying the definition of 'Subsidiary'. Originally, a 'Subsidiary' is defined as an entity in which a Party owns or controls 50% or more of the ownership interest. The modified text changes the ownership threshold in subsection (ii) to 25%. This directly contradicts sections relying on the 50% threshold for determining rights and obligations related to Subsidiaries, especially within Article II Section 2.01 where NTL needs to own or control more than fifty percent (50%) of the voting stock or interests in ETI.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 2.01 NTL Technology. Entrust shall be entitled to the benefit of the NTL -------------- intellectual property licenses specified in Exhibit A (the "NTL Technology") for -------------- so long as NTL effectively owns or controls more than fifty percent (50%) of the voting stock or interests in ETI.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of a 'Subsidiary' by lowering the ownership threshold from 50% to 25% in subsection (ii). This directly contradicts Section 2.01, which states that Entrust is entitled to the benefit of NTL Technology 'so long as NTL effectively owns or controls more than fifty percent (50%) of the voting stock or interests in ETI.' The lowered threshold introduces a conflict, as an entity could now be considered a subsidiary under the modified definition with only 25% ownership, while Section 2.01 explicitly requires more than 50% ownership for Entrust to receive the NTL Technology benefits. This creates an inconsistency in the contract, making it unclear whether Entrust is entitled to benefits if NTL owns between 25% and 50% of an entity.

perturbation.original_text: For so long as ETI is an NTL Subsidiary, NTL shall also be entitled, as part of its continuing Patent cross licensing program, to sublicense Entrust Patents under new Patent cross license agreements provided that the rights granted in the Entrust Patents pursuant to any such new Patent cross license agreements do not materially exceed those rights customarily granted under NTL's existing Patent cross license agreements (as of the Effective Date) and ETI obtains the benefit of all Nortel Patents involved.
perturbation.changed_text: For so long as ETI is an NTL Subsidiary, NTL shall also be entitled, as part of its continuing Patent cross licensing program, to sublicense Entrust Patents under new Patent cross license agreements provided that the rights granted in the Entrust Patents pursuant to any such new Patent cross license agreements do not materially exceed those rights customarily granted under NTL's existing Patent cross license agreements (as of the Effective Date) and **without any benefit to ETI for all Nortel Patents involved**.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text contradicts the original agreement by stating that ETI will not receive any benefit of all Nortel Patents involved. This introduces a contradiction to the overall intention of the agreement regarding mutual benefit and fair exchange, it is understood the ETI will receive the benefit for all Nortel Patents involved. Specifically, it contradicts the spirit and implied terms of Section 6.01, which provides ETI benefit from cross licenses. This change creates uncertainty in the enforcement of cross-licensing terms and could lead to disputes regarding the fairness and reciprocity of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 6.01. ETI Benefit from Cross Licenses. Subject to the terms and -------------------------------- conditions of this Agreement, NTL, to the extent of its legal right to do so, hereby grants to Entrust under the Nortel Patents, a non-transferable, non- assignable, indivisible, non-exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide license for Licensed Products and Licensed Services.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that ETI will receive 'without any benefit to ETI for all Nortel Patents involved,' while Section 6.01 states that 'NTL... hereby grants to Entrust under the Nortel Patents, a non-transferable, non- assignable, indivisible, non-exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide license for Licensed Products and Licensed Services.' This creates a direct contradiction because the changed text denies ETI the benefit that Section 6.01 explicitly grants, thus leading to uncertainty over the cross-licensing terms, which satisfies the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: Subject to early termination for material default, such Reseller Agreement shall expire either in three years or when ETI ceases to be a Subsidiary of NTL, whichever event occurs later.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to early termination for material default, such Reseller Agreement shall expire either in **one** years or when ETI ceases to be a Subsidiary of NTL, whichever event occurs later.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text modifies the expiration term of the Reseller Agreement from three years to one year, creating an in-text contradiction with other sections that rely on the three-year term. This introduces uncertainty and impacts the long-term resale rights and obligations initially agreed upon. It contradicts Article X Section 10.01 Term. Except as otherwise provided in this Agreement, this ---- Agreement shall terminate on the later of (i) the third anniversary of the Effective Date or (ii) the date on which ETI ceases to be a Subsidiary of NTL.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 10.01. Term. Except as otherwise provided in this Agreement, this ---- Agreement shall terminate on the later of (i) the third anniversary of the Effective Date or (ii) the date on which ETI ceases to be a Subsidiary of NTL.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the term of the Reseller Agreement from three years to one year. The contradicted text, Section 10.01, explicitly states that the agreement terminates on the 'third anniversary'. Therefore, the changed text directly contradicts Section 10.01 by creating a conflicting term length for the agreement, making it unclear which term is valid. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition as it provides conflicting deadlines.
